=

This receipt was already in the ledger and was not added again.

Receipt details
Vendor: {{ $('Merge & Stage: Receipt Row').item.json.vendor }}

parameters.content:
## 📩 Trigger: New Labeled Email
Watches Gmail for new emails carrying the `historical invoice` label. Whenever Tom labels a receipt, the workflow picks it up on the next poll (every minute). PDF attachments are downloaded automatically.

## 📄 Prepare Email Body
This step takes the email's subject, sender, date, and body and turns them into a clean format that easybits can read. Pure JavaScript, no external services needed.

## 🤖 Extract Receipt Details from Email Body
Sends the email body PDF to the **easybits** Extractor pipeline. easybits reads it and returns 6 structured fields: vendor, amount, currency, plan name, billing period, and invoice date.

## 🔗 Reattach the Original Attachment
The Extractor drops attachments after processing. This step reunites the extracted data with the original PDF attachment (if there was one) so the next step can check whether one exists.

## 🔀 Does the Email Have a PDF Attachment?
Some receipts have all the info in the email body. Others come as a PDF attachment with a short note in the email. This branch checks if there's a PDF attached and, if so, sends it through a second round of extraction.

## 📎 Prepare the PDF Attachment
Saves the email body's extraction results to the side (so they don't get lost), then picks out the first PDF attachment and hands it to the next step for a second round of extraction.

## 🤖 Extract Receipt Details from Attachment
Sends the PDF attachment to the same **easybits** pipeline. Returns the same 6 fields again – this time from the attachment's content. Useful as a backup in case the email body was light on details.

## 🧮 Combine Both Extractions
If both the email body and the attachment were processed, this step merges the two – preferring whatever the email body said, and falling back to the attachment for any missing fields. The result: one clean row ready for the Google Sheet.

## 🔍 Check if This Receipt is Already in the Ledger
Searches the Google Sheet for a row with the same email ID. This is the safety net: if Tom accidentally labels the same email twice, the workflow won't add it again.

## 🔀 New Receipt or Duplicate?
Routes the receipt down one of two paths – either it's new (add it to the ledger) or it's already there (send a heads-up email and skip).

## ✅ Add Receipt to the Ledger
Appends the receipt as a new row in the **Subscription Ledger** Google Sheet. This row becomes part of the baseline for future price comparisons.

## 🏷️ Remove Label
Strips the `historical invoice` label from the email so it isn't processed again on the next poll.

## 📧 Send Duplicate Notice
Sends Tom a short email letting him know this receipt was already in the ledger and wasn't added again. Keeps him in the loop without cluttering the Sheet.

## 🏷️ Remove Label
Strips the `historical invoice` label so the duplicate email isn't picked up again.

# 💸 Subscription Baseline Seeder (powered by easybits)

## What This Workflow Does
Tom's stack of AI tool subscriptions kept getting more expensive without him noticing. This workflow watches Gmail for subscription receipts that he labels `historical invoice`, reads the vendor, amount, plan, and date from each one (whether the info is in the email body or in a PDF attachment), and saves them all to a Google Sheet. This builds the **baseline** that the companion workflow – Subscription Price Drift Monitor – uses to detect price increases later.

Use this workflow once to seed the last few months of receipts per vendor, then leave the monitor running.

## How It Works
1. Tom labels a receipt email with `historical invoice` in Gmail
2. The email body is converted into a PDF the **easybits** Extractor can read
3. easybits pulls out vendor, amount, currency, plan, billing period, and date
4. If the email also has a PDF receipt attached, easybits reads that too – and any missing fields in the email body get filled in from the attachment
5. The workflow checks whether this receipt is already in the ledger
6. If new → it's added to the Google Sheet; if duplicate → a "skipped" notice goes to Tom's inbox
7. The Gmail label is removed so the email isn't processed again

## Setup Guide

### 1. Create the Gmail Label
In Gmail, create a label called `historical invoice` (lowercase, with the space). Apply it to the receipts you want to seed.

### 2. Set Up Your easybits Extractor Pipeline
1. Go to **extractor.easybits.tech**, sign up, and click **Create a Pipeline**
2. Name it `Subscription Receipt` and upload a sample receipt PDF as your reference document
3. Add these 6 fields manually with the descriptions provided in the **easybits Extractor docs** linked in the GitHub repo
4. Save & test the pipeline against your sample
5. Copy your **Pipeline ID** and **API Key**

### 3. Create the Google Sheet
1. Create a new Google Sheet called **Subscription Ledger**
2. Rename the first tab to `Receipts`
3. Add these column headers in row 1, in this exact order: `date` · `vendor` · `amount` · `currency` · `plan_name` · `billing_period` · `source_label` · `email_id`
4. Format column **A** (`date`) as Plain Text and column **C** (`amount`) as Number with 2 decimal places

### 4. Connect Credentials in n8n
1. **Gmail Trigger** – connect your Gmail OAuth and select the `historical invoice` label
2. **easybits nodes** – paste your API key and Pipeline ID into both Extractor nodes
3. **Google Sheets nodes** – connect your Google Sheets OAuth and point all three nodes at the **Subscription Ledger** sheet
4. **Gmail notification + label nodes** – confirm your Gmail OAuth is selected

### 5. Test and Activate
Run the workflow manually with one labeled receipt. Check the Google Sheet for a new row and confirm the label was removed. Once it works end-to-end, activate the workflow.

### 6. Seed Your Baseline
Label your last 2–3 receipts per vendor with `historical invoice`. The workflow will process them one by one as Gmail polls.

Once the baseline is seeded, switch over to the companion workflow – **Subscription Price Drift Monitor** – to start catching price increases automatically.
